description: This is the standard third-party Maven Wrapper bootstrap utility (MavenWrapperDownloader.java) executed only at build time via mvnw to fetch the Maven distribution — it is not part of the deployed application runtime and receives no untrusted network input. printStackTrace() here just logs a download failure to the console before System.exit(1), which is normal build-tool diagnostics, not exploitable debug code left in a shipped app.

This is the standard MavenWrapperDownloader.java build utility (not part of the deployed application runtime); printStackTrace() here just prints download errors to stdout/stderr for a local build-time script before System.exit(1), with no untrusted input reaching it or sensitive data exposed to remote users.

This is the standard Maven Wrapper downloader boilerplate, a build-time bootstrap script executed locally/in CI, not a deployed application handling untrusted input; printStackTrace() here just logs a download failure to stdout before exiting, with no sensitive data exposure or reachable attack surface.

This is the standard, widely-distributed Maven Wrapper bootstrap script (MavenWrapperDownloader.java), a build-time CLI utility that runs locally during the build process, not deployed application code reachable by untrusted input. printStackTrace() here just prints diagnostic output to console for a build tool invoked by a developer, before System.exit(1), which is normal for a standalone downloader utility, not exposed 'debug code' in a running service.

e.printStackTrace() is leftover debug code in production payment-processing logic that writes exception details (including stack traces) to stderr instead of using the application's logging framework; this matches the rule's intent even though the exception is still rethrown afterward.

e.printStackTrace() is leftover debug code left in a production catch block instead of proper logging, matching the rule's definition; it writes internal stack trace details (including exception messages that may contain sensitive payment-processing info) to stdout/stderr in a payment integration module, which is poor practice even though it isn't directly reflected to the client.

The code (based on the commented-out alternatives) instantiates AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding, uses a static IV constant (IV_P) rather than a random per-message IV, and provides no MAC/integrity check, which is the classic CBC padding-oracle and IV-reuse weakness; this is production encryption logic (EncryptionImpl), not test code, so it's a genuine defect if this cipher output is ever decrypted in a context that reveals padding-validity errors to an attacker.

The code explicitly instantiates Cipher.getInstance with a CBC/PKCS5Padding spec (confirmed by the commented-out NoPadding line above) and uses a static IV, making it vulnerable to padding oracle attacks and lacking integrity protection; this is genuine decryption logic, not test/mock code.

The code contains an actual e.printStackTrace() call left in a production catch block, matching the rule's exact target pattern of leftover debug code; it can leak stack trace details (internal paths, class names) to stderr/logs instead of proper logging. Impact is modest since output isn't directly returned to the client, but the defect class itself is genuinely present, not a false match.

This is the standard Maven Wrapper Downloader boilerplate, a build-time utility script (not deployed application code) that runs locally during the build to fetch the Maven wrapper jar; printStackTrace() here just logs a download failure to the console before exiting and exposes no sensitive data to untrusted/remote input.

This is the standard Maven Wrapper Downloader build script (executed by developers/CI to bootstrap Maven), not deployed application code reachable by untrusted end users; printStackTrace() here is ordinary build-time error diagnostics, not exploitable debug code exposed in a running service.

CSRF protection is explicitly disabled via .csrf().disable() for the /shop/** matcher, which includes authenticated, state-changing endpoints under /shop/customer/** requiring ROLE_AUTH_CUSTOMER. This exposes authenticated users to cross-site request forgery on customer actions unless CSRF is otherwise handled elsewhere (not shown here).

CSRF protection is explicitly disabled for /services/** while the same filter chain enables formLogin, which authenticates via session cookies; state-changing requests under /services/private/** (protected only by session/basic auth) can thus be forged from a malicious page since no CSRF token is required.

This filter chain protects a REST API (antMatcher `/private/**`) authenticated via HTTP Basic plus a custom bearer/token filter (authenticationTokenFilter), not cookie/session-based auth — since browsers don't automatically attach Basic/Bearer credentials to cross-site requests, CSRF is not applicable here, making disabling CSRF a standard and safe pattern for stateless token-authenticated APIs.

This filter chain protects /auth/** using httpBasic() authentication, whose credentials browsers automatically cache and resend on subsequent requests (similar to cookies), making CSRF a real risk; disabling CSRF here removes protection against forged cross-origin requests to authenticated endpoints under this matcher.

downloadFile is a read-only file-retrieval endpoint (returns bytes, no state mutation), so even though the HTTP method is unrestricted, there's no state-changing action for CSRF to exploit; the missing method restriction is a hardening/best-practice gap, not a genuine CSRF vulnerability.

The endpoint only reads a file from contentService and streams its bytes back (with a Content-Disposition header); it performs no state change, so classic CSRF impact doesn't apply even though the missing explicit RequestMethod technically allows all HTTP verbs. This is a common semgrep false-positive pattern where the rule flags any @RequestMapping lacking a method, regardless of whether the action is state-changing.

The handler only reads image files by path variables and returns bytes (a GET-style read operation with no state change), so lacking an explicit HTTP method does not create a CSRF risk even though RequestMapping defaults to allowing all methods.

The endpoint only reads and returns image bytes based on path variables (no state mutation visible), so while the missing HTTP method annotation is a real code-quality/hardening gap, CSRF (which targets state-changing requests) is not meaningfully exploitable here.

printImage only reads and returns image bytes based on path variables with no state-changing side effects, so even though the HTTP method isn't explicitly restricted, there is no CSRF-exploitable action here — the finding is a generic pattern match rather than a genuine vulnerability.

This endpoint only reads and returns a static product image file (byte[] response, no request body indicating state mutation); CSRF protections are only meaningful for state-changing operations, and this handler performs none, so the missing explicit method restriction is a code-quality/best-practice gap rather than an exploitable CSRF vector.

This @RequestMapping is on an @ExceptionHandler method inside a @ControllerAdvice class with no path/value mapping — it's invoked by Spring's exception-handling machinery when another controller throws, not as a directly-routable, state-changing endpoint reachable via attacker-controlled requests. The missing HTTP-method restriction is essentially cosmetic here since there's no state change or CSRF-sensitive action performed.

This is a read-only @ExceptionHandler that formats an error response for ServiceRuntimeException; it performs no state-changing operation, so lack of explicit HTTP method restriction poses no genuine CSRF risk even if the annotation combination is unusual.

This is an @ExceptionHandler method in a REST error-handling class that only formats and returns error metadata from an already-thrown exception; it performs no state-changing operation and has no reachable side effects, so the missing explicit HTTP method on @RequestMapping poses no genuine CSRF risk even though the pattern is flagged.

This is an @ExceptionHandler method in a REST error-handling class that formats exception details into an ErrorEntity response; it performs no state-changing operation and is invoked by Spring's exception resolution machinery rather than being a directly CSRF-exploitable state-mutating endpoint. The generic rule flags any @RequestMapping lacking an explicit method, but this handler is read-only/idempotent by nature.

This method is an @ExceptionHandler in what is almost certainly a @ControllerAdvice class, invoked internally by Spring's exception-resolution machinery rather than directly routable by arbitrary HTTP verbs; it also only reads exception data and returns an error body with no state-changing side effects, so CSRF risk from missing method restriction is not applicable.

This @RequestMapping is on an @ExceptionHandler method inside what is presumably a @ControllerAdvice error handler; it is not a directly-invokable endpoint reachable by arbitrary attacker-chosen HTTP method, but is instead dispatched internally by Spring when an exception is thrown during another request's handling. The method also performs no state change (only logging and building an error response), so there is no CSRF-exploitable action here despite the missing 'method' attribute.

e.printStackTrace() in a catch block is exactly the debug-code pattern the rule targets — it swallows a ServiceException while dumping the stack trace to stderr instead of proper logging, which is a genuine leftover debug artifact even though the trace isn't directly reflected to an HTTP client here.

e.printStackTrace() is used as production error handling in a service facade instead of a logger, exposing internal stack trace details to stderr/console and matching the exact debug-code pattern the rule targets; it's not test/mock code and the method swallows the exception while leaking diagnostic info.

printStackTrace() here only logs an UnknownHostException to stderr/console during local host resolution, with no untrusted input involved and no sensitive data exposed to an attacker; it's a code-quality/logging concern, not an exploitable vulnerability.

The catch block calls e.printStackTrace() directly, which is exactly the debug-code pattern the rule targets; this is production request-handling code (not test/mock), so the leftover debug statement is genuinely present and reachable, though its security impact is limited to log/stderr exposure rather than direct data leakage to the client.

The rule flags Triple DES (DESede) usage, but the code calls KeyGenerator.getInstance("DES"), which is plain single DES, not DESede — the rule's specific claim doesn't match the algorithm string shown, so this is a rule/code mismatch rather than a confirmed DESede finding (a separate weak-crypto concern about plain DES may still exist, but that is not what this rule asserts).

The code explicitly calls KeyGenerator.getInstance("DES") to generate a symmetric key used elsewhere for encryption/decryption (note the CIPHER constant nearby), and DES's 56-bit effective key space is broken by brute force with modern hardware, making this a genuine weak-cryptography defect regardless of the mismatched CIPHER string.

The cipher is initialized with only a key and no IV (aes.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key)), consistent with ECB mode (Java's AES default when no mode is specified is ECB), and the code encrypts data reachable via tokenizeString for use elsewhere in the app.
